metadata.summary: Accessibility law matrix 2026: ADA Title II (US April 2026), EAA (EU June 2025/2030), Section 508, AODA, UK/AU. Deadlines, WCAG standards, VPAT, enforcement, exemptions.

content.title: Regulatory Compliance 2026
content.summary: Regional accessibility law matrix for 2026: ADA Title II (US government, April 2026 deadline), European Accessibility Act (EU e-commerce + banking, June 2025/2030), Section 508, AODA (Canada), and equivalent frameworks in the UK, Australia, and Asia-Pacific. Covers deadlines, WCAG standard mappings, documentation requirements (VPAT/ACR, accessibility statements), enforcement penalties, and exemptions.

why.p: Non-compliance risks vary by region: US ADA lawsuits average $5,000-$75,000 in settlements plus $50,000-$200,000 in legal fees; EU EAA fines reach 4% of annual revenue. Regulations differ in scope, standard version (WCAG 2.0 vs 2.1 vs EN 301 549), and enforcement mechanism. A single compliance checklist does not cover all jurisdictions; organizations must identify which regulations apply and what documentation is required.

list.item: Determining which accessibility standard (WCAG 2.0/2.1/2.2, EN 301 549) applies to a given product and jurisdiction.
Writing or auditing accessibility statements, VPATs, and ACRs.
Setting up ongoing monitoring, training, and procurement standards for ADA/EAA compliance.
Assessing risk before a product launch in a new region.
Responding to a DOJ complaint or EU enforcement action.
Technical WCAG implementation — use `wcag-22-compliance` or `a11y-testing`.
AT runtime testing — use `testing-with-assistive-technology`.
XR/spatial products — apply WCAG + W3C XAUR; no dedicated regulation yet.
Internal-only tooling with no public-facing interface — most regulations apply to public-facing services only.

rule.statement: US government entities must comply with ADA Title II at WCAG 2.1 AA by April 24, 2026 (large entities, 50K+ population) or April 24, 2027 (smaller entities).
rule.rationale: DOJ final rule published March 2024. Non-compliance exposes state/local agencies to DOJ investigation, private lawsuits, and loss of federal funding.

example.title: US regulation matrix
example.code: Regulation | Scope | Deadline | Standard | Enforcement ADA Title II (DOJ) | State/local government | Apr 2026 (large) | WCAG 2.1 AA | DOJ + lawsuits ADA Title III | Private businesses (courts) | Ongoing | WCAG 2.0/2.1 AA | Private lawsuits Section 508 | Federal agencies | Now | WCAG 2.0 AA | Agency oversight CVAA | Video, communications | Now | WCAG 2.0 AA | FCC

section.name: european-union

rule.statement: EAA requires WCAG 2.1 AA (via EN 301 549) for new e-commerce, banking, transport, and e-book products from June 28, 2025; existing products have until June 28, 2030.
rule.rationale: EU member states set their own enforcement; fines up to 4% of annual revenue. Web Accessibility Directive already applies to all public-sector websites.

example.title: EU regulation matrix
example.code: Regulation | Scope | Deadline | Standard EAA | E-commerce, banking, transport, e-books | Jun 2025 (new) | EN 301 549 (~WCAG 2.1 AA) | | Jun 2030 (existing) | Web Accessibility Directive | Public-sector websites and apps | Now | EN 301 549

section.p: EAA explicitly covers: e-commerce websites, banking services (ATMs, web, mobile), electronic communications, transport (ticketing, apps), e-books, and consumer-facing services.

example.title: Global regulation matrix
example.code: Jurisdiction | Regulation | Standard | Status Canada (ON) | AODA | WCAG 2.0 AA | Now Canada (Fed) | ACA | WCAG 2.1 AA | Now / 2025 UK | Equality Act 2010 | WCAG 2.1 AA* | Now (*recommended) UK (public) | Public Sector Bodies Regs | WCAG 2.1 AA | Now Australia | Disability Discrimination Act | WCAG 2.1 AA | Now (recommended) Japan | JIS X 8341-3 | WCAG 2.0 based | Now Israel | IS 5568 | WCAG 2.0 AA | Now Brazil | e-MAG | WCAG 2.0 based | Now India | GIGW | WCAG 2.0+ | Government sites

rule.statement: Every public-facing product must publish an accessibility statement that includes conformance level, known limitations, contact information, and the date of the last review.
rule.rationale: Required by ADA Title II (DOJ rule), EU Web Accessibility Directive, and EAA. The statement is the primary compliance evidence regulators and plaintiffs request first.

list.item: Conformance level stated (e.g., "Partially conformant with WCAG 2.1 Level AA").
Known limitations listed — vague "ongoing effort" language is not sufficient.
Contact mechanism for accessibility issues (email, phone, or form).
Date of last statement update.
Feedback/complaint process described.
Alternative access methods noted if applicable.

rule.statement: Produce a Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT) — also called an Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR) — for every product used in government or enterprise procurement; update annually or after major changes.
rule.rationale: US Section 508 and ADA Title II procurement standards require VPAT evidence. EU public sector procurement increasingly requires EN 301 549 ACRs. Without one, vendors are excluded from bids.

list.item: All WCAG criteria addressed (pass / partial / not supported / not applicable).
Evidence of conformance cited per criterion.
Known issues documented with remediation timeline.
Updated annually or on major version changes.

rule.statement: Organizations must maintain an internal accessibility policy documenting organizational commitment, responsible parties, testing cadence, training requirements, and procurement standards.
rule.rationale: DOJ consent decrees and EU member state enforcement demand evidence of systemic process, not one-off fixes. Policy is the paper trail that proves ongoing effort.

list.item: Responsible parties identified (owner, team).
Testing cadence: automated CI, quarterly manual audits, annual comprehensive audit.
Training requirements per role (dev, designer, content, procurement).
Procurement standards — VPAT required from all software/service vendors.
Complaint and escalation process.

example.title: US enforcement and penalties
example.code: Consequence | Typical Range Private lawsuit settlement | $5,000 - $75,000+ Legal fees (plaintiff) | $50,000 - $200,000+ Consent decree + monitoring | Multi-year compliance program DOJ investigation | Reputational + remediation cost Loss of federal funding | For government entities
example.note: Thousands of ADA web lawsuits filed annually. Serial plaintiffs target e-commerce, banking, and healthcare.

example.title: EU enforcement and penalties
example.code: Consequence | Notes Fine up to 4% of annual revenue | Per member state enforcement Product withdrawal from market | If non-conformant after deadline Prohibition of services | Regulator order Compensation to affected individuals | Civil action

list.item: Canada: Complaints to CRTC or provincial accessibility commissioners.
UK: Unlawful discrimination claims under Equality Act 2010.
Australia: Complaints to Australian Human Rights Commission.

rule.statement: Exemptions are narrow, must be individually documented, and always require an accessible alternative to be provided.
rule.rationale: Regulators and courts reject blanket exemptions. Each exemption needs a written justification, scope limitation, and alternative access path.

list.item: Pre-existing content — content published before the compliance deadline and not subsequently altered.
Archived content — clearly marked as archived, not updated, not relied on for active services.
Third-party content not under organizational control — must still provide an accessible alternative.
Undue burden — must document why compliance is an undue financial or administrative burden AND provide an alternative.
Fundamental alteration — compliance would change the fundamental nature of the service; must be documented and alternative provided.

antipattern.description: Claiming "undue burden" without a documented cost analysis and alternative access plan.
antipattern.reason: Courts and regulators reject undocumented exemptions; the burden of proof is on the organization claiming the exemption.

list.item: Aim at WCAG 2.2 AA now even though regulation cites 2.1 — minimal cost to over-deliver, easier to defend.
Publish the Accessibility Statement at a stable URL (`/accessibility`); link from footer on every page.
Treat VPAT-ACR as a living document, refresh on major releases or annually whichever is sooner.
Keep evidence-of-conformance: dated test reports, AT logs, user-test recordings (with consent) — discoverable in lawsuit.
Insert accessibility into procurement: VPAT required, remediation timelines in contract, audit rights.
Train every developer + designer + content owner annually; track completion (regulators ask).
When a complaint arrives, respond inside 48 hours with substantive engagement — settlement leverage rests on response history.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents drive the *paperwork* — accessibility statements, VPAT-ACR drafts, procurement-clause libraries, training-tracking spreadsheets, and quarterly-audit calendars — and a regulatory-compliance triage that maps a product's markets to a deadline matrix. They do **not** make compliance determinations; final sign-off is legal counsel + accessibility lead. Pair with `wcag-22-compliance` for the technical criteria checklist and `testing-with-assistive-technology` for evidence-of-conformance.

Regulatory landscape changes mid-year — DOJ rule clarifications, EAA member-state transpositions, new state laws (CA AB 1757, NY S6047). The README's table can drift; agents must verify dates from primary sources before use. "WCAG 2.1 AA" is the floor in most rules, but some courts apply WCAG 2.2 by default — under-specifying a deliverable target invites trouble. Exemptions (undue burden, fundamental alteration, archived content) are narrow and fact-specific; agents over-claim them. US ADA Title III still has no formal regulation for private businesses; case law (Robles v. Domino's) governs — agents cannot reason from rules alone. Procurement clauses must be mirror-tight; LLM-drafted contract language without legal review is a liability.

list.item: `vpat-tools` (community) — Markdown ↔ VPAT 2.5 templates.
`axe-core` + `pa11y-ci` — Generate evidence-of-conformance test reports.
`lighthouse-ci` — Accessibility scores attached to PR + budgets.
Pandoc + Word/PDF templates — Render Accessibility Statement / VPAT to required formats.
OPA (Open Policy Agent) — Encode procurement / accessibility policy as code.
GitHub Actions / GitLab CI — Wire continuous a11y monitoring evidence.
Deque axe Auditor / Manage (SaaS) — Tracks remediation against WCAG / EN 301 549.
SiteImprove (SaaS) — Enterprise compliance crawl + reporting.
Level Access (SaaS + services) — Managed compliance program; agents draft for counsel review.
TPGi ARC / JAWS Inspect (SaaS) — JAWS-led testing evidence.
Fable Tech Labs (SaaS) — Real-user evidence for VPAT remarks.
Agents conflate ADA Title II (public sector) and Title III (private) — keep them separate.
LLMs cite outdated standards (WCAG 2.0 AA) by training-data inertia; force WCAG 2.2 AA in every prompt.
Generated VPAT remarks tend to overstate conformance — require "Partially Supports" / "Does Not Support" justifications grounded in tests.
Exemption claims (undue burden, fundamental alteration) get inserted casually; force agents to cite the specific § and provide rationale.
Region coverage drifts (Quebec law differs from federal Canada; CA AB 1757 specifics; Texas HB 4090) — agents miss state/provincial nuance.
Penalty figures are quoted as gospel ($75,000 first offense, $150,000 subsequent for ADA) — those are DOJ ranges, not damages from private suits; agents conflate.
Human-in-loop checkpoints: every published Accessibility Statement, every VPAT shipped to a customer, every procurement clause, every response to a complaint or demand letter.

template.purpose: Accessibility statement template covering conformance status, known limitations, contact, and date.
